content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 25804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662         322-5660              322-5901              322-6441              322-6444

November 22, 1982

Conrad Lee Klein
14003 Ventura Boulevard
Sherman Oaks, CA 91423

RE:  FPPC No. GC-82/248

Dear Mr. Klein:

Thank you for your letter of November 11, 1982 containing
the information we requested as to Mr. Felton's horse racing
interests. According to your letter and Mr. Felton's Statement
of Economic Interest (SEI) for the year 1981, Mr. Felton has an
ownership interest of between $10,000 and $100,000 in eight
different business entities that breed and/or race racehorses.
Mr. Felton's SEI also shows that he received income in 1981 from
four of those business entities in amounts between $1,000 and
$10,000.

As a member of the Commission on California State Government
Organization and Economy (the Commission), Mr. Felton was the
lone dissenting vote of eight voting Commissioners in the
adoption of the Commission's July 1982 report, "Horse Racing in
California: Revenue and Regulation." Among other things, that
report recommended that the legislature reconsider its action on
Assembly Bill 3383, which became law on January 1, 1981. The
focus of the bill was a redistribution of horseracing revenues,
giving a larger share to racing associations, breeders, and
owners while decreasing the State's share in the racing
revenues. Although no specific reforms are listed in the report,
the Commission presents data showing that investors in
horseracing associations receive a significantly greater return
on the equity than investors in other industries. This finding,
along with the Commission's estimate of at least a $25 million
reduction in State revenues in 1981 as a result of AB 3383,
supports the Commission's implied conclusion that the
distribution of horseracing revenues should be restructured
again, this time to the benefit of the State at the expense of
the horseracing interests.

4-82/194

Mr. Klein
November 22, 1982
Page 2

The Political Reform Act (the Act) at Government Code
Section 87100_1/ provides that:

No public official at any level of state or local government
shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to
use his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest.

Section 87103 indicates that an official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on:

(1) a business in which the official has an investment
worth     more than $1,000,

(2) real property in which the official has an interest
worth more than $1,000,

(3) any source of income to the official of $250 or more,

(4) any business in which the official is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position
of management.

In Commission on California State Government Organization
and Economy v. F.P.P.C. 75 Cal. App. 3d 716 (1977), the court
found that the "advisory" Commission was not exempt from the
conflict of interest provisions of the Act; the court said the
Commission had decisionmaking authority based on its
investigatory and contractual powers. Accordingly, the
Commission was required to promulgate a Conflict of Interest Code
and its members were required to file SFI's. Therefore, although
in the present case, the Commission is "recommending" that the
legislature reconsider AB 3383, this action is a governmental
decision within the purview of Section 87100.

The minutes of the Commission meeting of July 20, 1982 show
that Mr. Felton was given a choice of abstaining or dissenting
from the Commission's adoption of the horseracing report. The
fact that Mr. Felton chose to dissent instead of abstaining
indicates that he intended to show his rejection of the report

_______________________

1/ All statutory references are to the California
Government Code unless otherwise noted.

Mr. Klein
November 22, 1982
Page 3

rather than to disqualify himself from a conflict of interest
situation. Moreover, this limited action by Mr. Felton is
sufficient to qualify as participating in making, or using his
official position to influence, a governmental decision. The
FPPC's interpretive regulation at 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700,
specifically provides that an official voting on a matter has
made a governmental decision. Regulation 18700(c) also lists
those activities prohibited because they involve participating in
the making of a governmental decision: participating in
deliberations or negotiations, advising or making substantive
recommendations to the decision maker, preparing or presenting
any report, analysis or opinion which requires the exercise of
judgment and is intended to influence the decision. Thus, if Mr.
Felton had given the Commission his own views on the horseracing
industry, as he suggested, at the July 20 meeting, such action
could also have been a potential violation of Section 87100.

Turning to Mr. Felton's financial interests, his disclosure
of his horseracing investments and income on his SEI's indicates
that his investments are not "minor" nor is his income
"negligible." 2 Cal Adm. Code Section 18702 sets forth
guidelines for use in determining the materiality of any
financial effect on an official's interests as a result of the
governmental decision. Among other things, the financial effect
will be material if:

(1) The annualized gross revenues of a business in which
the official holds an investment of $1,000 or more, will
increase or decrease by the lesser of one percent or
$100,000,

(2) The effect of the decision will be to directly
increase or decrease the amount of income to be received by
the official by one hundred dollars or more, or,

(3) There is a nexus between the governmental decision and
the purpose for which the official receives income.

The Commission's report shows that the pari-mutuel take out
for owners and breeders increased by 35.10% in 1981 from the
previous year after the passage of AB 3383. Applying such a
percentage change to Mr. Felton's business entities would clearly
demonstrate a material financial effect on the business' gross
revenues as well as on Mr. Felton's income from those
businesses. Moreover, the report Mr. Felton voted on directly
addressed the issue of return on horseracing investors' equity.
Therefore there was a nexus between the governmental decision and
the reason Mr. Felton received income.

Mr. Klein
November 22, 1982
Page 4


     In addition to materiality, the effect on an official's
financial interest must be reasonably foreseeable and
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally to find a
conflict of interest.  Foreseeability is judged subjectively on a
case by case basis.  See Thorner, Tom, Marin Municipal Water
District I FPPC 198 (No. 75-089, Dec. 4, 1975).  In Mr. Felton's
situation, the effect will occur by the Legislature doing what
the Commission recommended:  restructuring the division of
horseracing revenues.  When one considers the research supporting
the Commission's conclusion, the authoritative prestige of the
Commission, and the State's current budget deficit, it is
reasonably foreseeable that some restructuring of the revenues
could occur.

     The FPPC's interpretive regulation at 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18703 provides that an industry, trade or profession does
not constitute a significant segment of the general public.  An
exception to this rule is when the statute creating the
official's agency declares that certain industry representatives
should serve on the agency to further the interests of the
industry as well as the public.  In that situation, those
industry representatives will be considered to constitute the
public generally.  See Callanan, Sands and Hill 4 FPPC 33 (No.
77-036, April 5, 1978).  Here, there is no such finding by the
Legislature that any particular industry representatives will be
considered as the public generally for purposes of serving on the
Commission.  Your reference to racehorse owners and breeders
being tantamount to the public generally (Business and
Professions Code Section 19424.5) is taken out of context as that
finding applies only to members of the California Horseracing
Board.  Consequently, the effect of the Commission's governmental
decision on Mr. Felton's financial interests, is distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally.

     In conclusion, the elements necessary to find a conflict of
interest under the provisions of the Act are all present in the
case of Mr. Felton.  While it appears that no one, including the
Commission's Executive Director, advised Mr. Felton that he might
be voting in a conflict of interest situation, it should be noted
that the disqualification and disclosure provisions apply to each
public official individually.  However, as you point out in the
final paragraph of your November 11 letter, Mr. Felton did not
appear to intentionally violate the Act by casting his dissenting
vote; the minutes of the particular Commission meeting show that
there was some confusion on Mr. Felton's part as to
disassociating himself from the horseracing report.  Therefore,
since Mr. Felton's vote was an isolated incident and his conduct
was negligent at most, we have determined not to initiate a

Mr. Klein
November 22, 1982
Page 5


formal enforcement action against Mr. Felton, see Section
91001(c). However, this letter will serve as notice to Mr.
Felton that he must disqualify himself from participating in
similar governmental decisions in the future. Failure to do so
will show an absence of good faith and may require a formal
enforcement remedy.

     Thank you for your cooperation in this matter and please do
not hesitate to contact either myself or Alan Herndon if you have
any questions regarding this letter. Questions regarding other
conflict of interest matters should be directed to our Legal
Division at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Steven R. Crooks
                                        Legal Counsel
                                        Enforcement Division

SRC:lh

Law Offices of
Conrad Lee Klien

Conrad Lee Klien
Of Counsel
Harold Steinberg
Lt Verne A Wardlaw

Conrad Lee Klien
14003 Ventura Boulevard
Berman Oaks, California 91423

Telephone
Stow Oaks 213
988-5800

November 11, 1982

Mr. Al Herndon
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Benjamin Felton

Dear Mr. Herndon and Commissioners:

Thank you for your patience in waiting for this
letter of information which you have requested regarding
Commissioner Ben Felton of the commission on California
State Government Organization and Economy (the "Commission").

I will attempt to address your concerns, as I perceive them, based on information I have obtained from
Mr. Felton, from you, from the California Horse Racing
Board, from my own knowledge and experience, and from
others.

It appears that the thrust of your inquiry relates
to Mr. Felton's qualified "dissent", on July 20, 1982,
to the adoption by the Commission of a study/report of
a subcommittee of the Commission entitled "Horse Racing
in California: Revenue and Regulation".

Mr. Felton's act would not appear to constitute
participation by him to influence a government decision,
which decision would be reasonably foreseeable to have a
material financial effect on any interest he had. (This
conclusion is true whether tested by hindsight or foresight.)

The subject study covered about twenty five (25)
aspects of parimutual horse racing conducted in California
and the activities of the California Horse Racing Board
in regulating such racing.

Mr. Al Herndon
November 11, 1982
Page Two

Commissioner Felton, a person having some minor
horse breeding interests voted "Nay" after the other
seven members had voted "Aye" to accept the report by
the Commission of its subcommittee.... five "Aye's" were
sufficient to carry the motion. (See Exhibit "A" for
dialogue regarding the vote.)

Aside from the fact that Mr. Felton's income as
a breeder has always been and still is negligible (See
Exhibit "B") his ineffectual "vote" on whether to accept
the subcommittee's report, which report was neither for
nor against horsemen (See Exhibit "C") could hardly be
deemed to satisfy the statutory preconditions for imposing
penalties.

In fact, Mr. Felton's objections to the report of
the subcommittee had nothing to do with anything that was
in the subcommittee's study, rather he merely voted to
record that he felt that there were problems in horse
racing which the report had not addressed and to which
he, Mr. Felton, would be willing to speak. In response
to Mr. Felton's offer, the Commission Chairman called
for a vote on the motion of adopting the report. (See
Exhibit "A".)

After the vote, above discussed, Mr. Felton said
he wanted his name deleted as a "signatory" to the report,
clearly a well directed goal. In response to this request,
Commissioner Post commented that Mr. Felton could abstain
or which point the Commission's Executive Director corrected
Mr. Post by telling Mr. Felton that he could either abstain
or dissent and an asterisk would be placed after his name.
Based thereon, Mr. Felton chose the latter alternative.

It would seem that Mr. Felton's limited activities
with the respect to the adoption of the study/report by a
subcommittee of the Commission could hardly be deemed to
constitute the making or the participation in making or
otherwise attempting to use his official position to in-
fluence a governmental decision in which he knew or had
reason to know that he had a financial interest, in that
the adoption, or the non-adoption by the Commission of
the subcommittee report on its study of horse racing, had
no financial effect on any person or entity, certainly
not on breeders since the study did not purport to come

Mr. Al Herndon
November 11, 1982
Page Three


to any conclusion or even to "offer a definitive comment"
because of the sub-committee's lack of information regarding horsemen's financial needs.  (See excerpt from
page 10 of subcommittee's study in Exhibit "C", with
emphasis added.)

     Even if one could hypothesize a scenario by which
the adoption of this report would be deemed a decision
which could conceivably affect Mr. Felton, the test is
not of remote possibility but rather it must be reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect.

     Furthermore, if one might be inclined to conclude
that the subject decision would have the requisite material
financial effect on Mr. Felton's interests, it would not
be distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
and thus, no violative of Government Code Section 87100.
As stated by the Legislature in 1977:

     "...the Legislature finds racehorse owners and
breeders are tantamount to and constitute the
public generally within the meaning of Section
87103 of the Government Code." (Stats. 1977,
ch. 1080, Section 7; emphasis added.)

     Finally in this regard, even if we make a quantum
leap to the conclusion that conceivably there may be a
technical violation of Government Code Section 87100, no
rational person could conclude that Mr. Felton's acts
were other than in absolute good faith, and truly inadvertant or negligent, and in no way calculated, designed
or intended to obtain a scintilla of financial gain and,
accordingly, pursuant to subparagraph (c) of Government
Code Section 91001, the remedies and sanctions of Title 9
of the Government Code should not be applied to Mr. Felton...
neither should an agency such as the Fair Political Practices
Commission which should and does engage in constructive
activities designed to protect the public, squander its
limited resources on such an insignificant matter.

     De Minimis Non Curat Lex!

     Thank you.
                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        CONRAD LEE KLEIN

CLK/a
Enclosure

SHAPELL:  The Commission is now ready to act on the final
            draft of Horse Racing Board study.  I shall entertain
            a motion.

TRUGMAN:   I make a motion that we accept the report of the
            Committee chaired by Commissioner Post and Commissioner
            Walker as co-commissioner on this subcommittee and
            also to compliment them on their hard work and final
            preparation of a very fine report.

FELTON:    Excuse me, Mr. Chairman, I would like to go on
            record to dissent from the report.  I cannot......

SHAPELL:   First we need a second.

GERSTEN:   Second.

SHAPELL:   Any discussion on this motion.

FELTON:    I feel the report does not reflect correctly the
            total picture of horse racing.  There are a lot of
            problems in horse racing but I just feel that there
            are a lot of factual problems here that have not
            been addressed.  I could take about a half and hour
            if the Commission wants to hear it.

SHAPELL:   We have a question on the motion, please.  Call for
            the question.  All in favor.

            AYES:  Alquist, Chalker, Walker, Gersten, Post,
            Trugman, and Shapell.

            NAY:   Felton.

FELTON:    For the record though, if we are going to be signa-
            tories I don't want my name.....

POST:      No, you abstain.

HALCOMB:   We can put on an asterisk behind your name, Ben, that
            you abstain or dissent.

FELTON:    I dissent.

HALCOMB:   All right.


                         Exhibit "A"

Set forth below is information concerning Mr. Felton's horse
interests for 1981 and 1982. All of these interests are
thorobreds and do not include any other breeds of horses.

1. The percentage of the combined interests of Mr. Felton
and of Mrs. Felton in the various entities is 33 1/3%
with the exception of Mr. & Mrs. Ben Felton Stable
which is 100% and West Valley Thorobred Breeders which
is 37½%.

2. The extent of horses in each of the partnerships is
as follows:

a) West Valley Thorobred Breeders consists of 1
brood mare 14 years old, and 1 yearling colt.

b) Shel-Ben Capital Co. consists of 2 brood mares
which are each 7 years old, and 1 baby horse
which is approximately 6 months old.

c) Sid-Ben Capital consists of 1 horse who is 5
years old which is due to be retired as a brood
mare in 1983.

d) Mr. & Mrs. Ben Felton Stable consists of 1
brood mare which is 3 years old, and a 2 year
old colt.

e) Distant Land breeding group consists of 1
stallion of which there are 36 share holders;
the Feltons own 1/3 of 1 share.

f) Fairraki Syndicate - Same as for Distant Land.

g) Mr. Cockatoo Breeding Syndicate - Feltons own 1
share outright - cost of the share was $1,000,
and present value of the share is $1,000 or less.

h) Penn's Friends Partnership - Consisted of one
5-year old horse which was sold at auction July
of 1982 and netted the partnership approximately
$800, of which Feltons received 1/3 (approximately
$267).

3. In connection with breeders awards as they relate to the
subject matter under inquiry, in 1981/82, Mr. & Mrs. Ben
Felton Stable received the sum of $485, and in 1981,
they received from West Valley Thorobred Breeders the
sum of $96. In 1982, they received the sum of $570
from West Valley Thorobred Breeders. (These breeders
awards figures are based on information furnished by
the California Thorobred Breeders Association as of
September 27, 1982; there may be some minor breeders
awards of $40 or $50 which are not here accounted for.

Exhibit "B"

EXCERPT FROM HORSE RACING STUDY/REPORT:

"According to the above Table, AB 3383 substantially
increased the revenues apportioned to racing associations
and horsemen. The logic behind this dramatic gift was
ostensibly,based upon a 1979 study performed by Temple, Barker
and Sloan 5/ (An Analysis of the California Horse Racing
Industry, March 1979) at the request of the CHRB and the
Racing Association. This report, funded by the Associations,
determined that without substantial financial relief horsemen
and racing associations could not continue their involvement
in horse racing."

"We attempted to review the report's assumptions
relative to the financial condition of horsemen. Using
their assumptions, we were able to recreate their findings.
In order to verify the horsemen's actual financial conditions,
however, we would need access to audited financial statements.
We were unable to obtain these and consequently cannot offer
a definitive comment on the horsemen's financial needs."


5/ Temple, Barker & Sloan is a consulting firm located
in Lexington, Massachusetts


Exhibit "C"
